# FedEx Shopify App — Agile Card

## User Story
As a merchant, I want the app to retrieve and display the updated shipping address after I modify it in Shopify and cancel a previously generated label, so that I can regenerate an accurate shipping label with the correct delivery location.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: Happy Path — Address Update Reflected After Label Cancellation
**Given** a label has been generated and the order fulfilled with an original address  
**When** the merchant cancels the label and updates the shipping address in Shopify  
**And** the merchant navigates to regenerate the shipping label  
**Then** the app displays the updated address from Shopify (not the cached original address)  
**And** the label can be generated successfully with the new address  

### Scenario 2: Address Update Without Label Cancellation
**Given** a label has been generated for an order  
**When** the merchant updates the shipping address in Shopify without cancelling the label  
**And** the merchant attempts to regenerate a label  
**Then** the app retrieves the latest address from Shopify  
**And** displays the updated address in the label creation form  

### Scenario 3: Multiple Address Updates
**Given** a label was generated with Address A  
**When** the merchant updates to Address B, cancels the label, then updates to Address C  
**And** the merchant regenerates the label  
**Then** the app displays Address C (the most recent update)  
**And** the label is generated with Address C  

### Scenario 4: Address Update with Partial Data
**Given** a label was generated with a complete address  
**When** the merchant updates only the street address or zip code in Shopify  
**And** the merchant regenerates the label  
**Then** the app displays all updated fields correctly  
**And** validation passes for the complete updated address  

### Scenario 5: No Address Update — Original Address Persists
**Given** a label was generated with an address  
**When** the merchant cancels the label without updating the address in Shopify  
**And** the merchant regenerates the label  
**Then** the app displays the original address  
**And** the label can be generated with the original address  

### Scenario 6: Address Update Fails or Sync Delay
**Given** the merchant updates the address in Shopify  
**When** there is a delay or failure in syncing the updated address to the app  
**And** the merchant attempts to regenerate the label immediately  
**Then** the app either displays a loading state or retrieves the address fresh from Shopify  
**And** does not display stale cached data  

### Scenario 7: Invalid Updated Address
**Given** a label was generated with a valid address  
**When** the merchant updates the address in Shopify with invalid data (missing required fields, invalid format)  
**And** the merchant attempts to regenerate the label  
**Then** the app displays the invalid address as entered in Shopify  
**And** shows a validation error message indicating which fields are missing or invalid  
**And** prevents label generation until the address is corrected  

## Priority
**High** — This directly impacts order fulfillment accuracy and customer satisfaction; merchants cannot fulfill corrected orders without a workaround.

## Test Scope
- **Orders Grid** — Verify address display and refresh behavior when regenerating labels
- **Label Domestic/International** — Confirm updated address is populated in label creation forms
- **Single Label** — Test address retrieval and display for single label regeneration
- **Settings** — Verify any caching or address sync settings are configured correctly
- **Notifications** — Confirm no stale address notifications are sent
- **Automation files:**
  - Address retrieval and caching logic
  - Label cancellation workflow
  - Shopify order sync/webhook handlers
  - Label regeneration form population
  - Address validation and error handling

## Out of Scope
- Mobile / responsive / viewport testing (web/desktop only)
- Changes to Shopify's address management UI
- Bulk address updates across multiple orders in a single operation
- Address standardization or USPS/FedEx address validation services
- Historical address audit trail or version control
- Automatic address correction without merchant confirmation

## References
- [PR #790 — BitBucket](https://bitbucket.org/xadapter-cyd/shopifyfedexapp/pull-requests/790)
- [Zendesk Ticket #361270](https://pluginhive.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/361270)

# FedEx Shopify App: Support Products with More Than 250 Variants

## User Story
As a Shopify merchant, I want to import and manage products with more than 250 variants, so that I can fulfill orders for all product variants without losing data or functionality.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: Product with exactly 250 variants (happy path)
**Given** a Shopify product has exactly 250 variants  
**When** the product is synced to the FedEx app  
**Then** all 250 variants are successfully imported and available for shipping label creation

### Scenario 2: Product with 251-500 variants (pagination - page 1)
**Given** a Shopify product has 300 variants  
**When** the product is synced to the FedEx app  
**Then** the GraphQL query paginates through all variants and imports all 300 variants successfully

### Scenario 3: Product with 1000+ variants (multiple pagination pages)
**Given** a Shopify product has 1,200 variants  
**When** the product is synced to the FedEx app  
**Then** the GraphQL query paginates through all pages and imports all 1,200 variants without data loss

### Scenario 4: Webhook triggers for products with >100 variants (feature flag enabled)
**Given** the feature flag `all.myshopify.com.shopify.webhook.products.with.more.than.100.variants.enabled` is `true`  
**And** the store creation date is after `2000-01-01`  
**When** a product with >100 variants is created or updated in Shopify  
**Then** the webhook is triggered and the product is synced with all variants paginated correctly

### Scenario 5: Webhook triggers for products with >100 variants (feature flag disabled)
**Given** the feature flag `all.myshopify.com.shopify.webhook.products.with.more.than.100.variants.enabled` is `false`  
**When** a product with >100 variants is created or updated in Shopify  
**Then** the webhook is not triggered and the product sync follows standard behavior

### Scenario 6: Store created before feature flag date threshold
**Given** a store was created before `2000-01-01`  
**And** the feature flag `shopify.webhook.products.with.more.than.100.variants.enabled.after.store.createdAt.greater.than` is set  
**When** a product with >100 variants is synced  
**Then** the webhook behavior respects the date threshold and does not trigger

### Scenario 7: Variant pagination with cursor-based navigation
**Given** a product has 500 variants  
**When** the GraphQL query uses cursor-based pagination to fetch variants  
**Then** each page returns the correct page size and cursor for the next page until all variants are retrieved

### Scenario 8: Error handling - GraphQL timeout during pagination
**Given** a product has 800 variants requiring multiple pagination requests  
**When** a GraphQL request times out during pagination  
**Then** the app logs the error, retries the failed page, and notifies the user of the sync status

### Scenario 9: Error handling - Incomplete variant import
**Given** a product has 600 variants  
**When** pagination fails midway (e.g., at page 2 of 3)  
**Then** the app does not mark the sync as complete and allows manual retry without duplicating previously imported variants

### Scenario 10: Variant data consistency after pagination
**Given** a product with 300 variants is imported via pagination  
**When** the variants are used to create shipping labels  
**Then** all variant SKUs, weights, and dimensions are correctly associated with their respective variants

## Priority
**High** — Products with >250 variants cannot currently be imported, blocking merchants from fulfilling orders for a significant portion of their catalog and limiting app usability for high-variant product businesses.

## Test Scope
- **Orders Grid** — Verify variants from paginated products appear correctly in order selection
- **Single Label** — Test label creation for variants from products with >250 variants
- **Rate Domestic/International** — Ensure rate calculations work for all variants from paginated products
- **Label Domestic/International** — Confirm labels can be generated for any variant from high-variant products
- **Settings** — Validate feature flag configuration and date threshold logic
- **Notifications** — Test sync status notifications for products with >250 variants
- **Bulk Orders** — Verify bulk operations work with variants from paginated products
- **GraphQL Integration** — Unit and integration tests for pagination cursor logic, page size handling, and error recovery
- **Webhook Processing** — Test webhook triggers with feature flag conditions and store creation date filtering
- **Product Sync** — End-to-end tests for variant import with various product variant counts (250, 251, 500, 1000+)

## Out of Scope
- Mobile / responsive / viewport testing (web/desktop only)
- UI/UX redesign for variant selection interfaces
- Performance optimization beyond pagination implementation
- Backward compatibility with products already partially imported with <250 variants
- Shopify Plus or custom app tier-specific features
- Variant filtering or search functionality (separate feature)

## References
- [BitBucket PR #791](https://bitbucket.org/xadapter-cyd/shopifyfedexapp/pull-requests/791)

# Card: Fix Rate Spike Post-REST Migration — #383117

## User Story
As a merchant who migrated from SOAP to REST, I want the same negotiated FedEx rates I had before migration, so that my shipping costs don't spike unexpectedly and I don't lose customers to sticker shock at checkout.

---

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

### FedEx REST API Rate Request Requirements
- **Account Number Requirement:** FedEx REST API requires explicit `accountNumber` in rate request payload to retrieve negotiated/contract rates. Omission or incorrect account number causes API to return retail list rates instead.
- **Rate Type Parameter:** REST API `rateType` parameter must be correctly set to retrieve contract pricing (vs. list rates). SOAP and REST implementations may differ in how this parameter is constructed or validated.
- **Negotiated Rate Eligibility:** Only FedEx accounts with active contract pricing agreements return negotiated rates; retail accounts always return list rates.
- **Rate Response Format Change:** FedEx changed Freight rate response format in their API (documented in PluginHive FAQ), which may affect how the app parses and applies rates across service types.

### PluginHive / App Behaviour
- **SOAP vs REST Rate Discrepancy:** Post-REST migration, merchants report rate spikes. Root cause hypothesis: REST API calls missing or incorrectly passing `accountNumber` or `rateType` parameters, causing fallback to retail list rates.
- **Fallback Rates:** App has separate fallback rate configuration for Domestic, International, and Freight (Settings → Rate Settings → Fallback Services). Fallback rates should NOT be returned when FedEx API is reachable and merchant has valid account.
- **Multiple Warehouse Support:** Each warehouse location can have its own FedEx account with independent rates. Rate request must use the correct account number for the selected warehouse.
- **Rate Comparison Logging:** Diagnostic logging capability (T003) will capture REST API request/response for rate discrepancy diagnosis. This AC assumes logging infrastructure is available or will be implemented as prerequisite.

### Known Unknowns (Open Questions)
- **Root Cause Not Yet Confirmed:** Is the issue missing `accountNumber` in REST payload, incorrect `rateType` parameter, or FedEx account configuration on merchant side?
- **SOAP vs REST Parameter Mapping:** How do SOAP and REST implementations differ in constructing rate request parameters? Are all SOAP fields correctly mapped to REST equivalents?
- **FedEx Partner Escalation Path:** If root cause is FedEx account configuration, what is the escalation process to FedEx partner channel?

---

## Prerequisites

### Test Data Setup
- **FedEx Test Account (Negotiated Rates):** A FedEx test account with active negotiated Ground rates configured in the app; account number stored in warehouse location settings.
- **FedEx Test Account (Retail Only):** A FedEx test account with no contract rates (retail account) for edge case testing.
- **Invalid Account Scenario:** Test account number intentionally omitted or set to invalid value in app settings.
- **Multiple Warehouse Setup:** Two warehouse locations, each with a different FedEx account number and independent negotiated rates.
- **SOAP Baseline Reference:** Known SOAP rate response for test shipment (Ground, 2-Day, Priority Overnight) with negotiated rates; used as baseline for REST parity validation.

### Diagnostic Logging Infrastructure
- Logging system must be capable of capturing and storing REST API request/response payloads, timestamps, merchant account identifiers, and warehouse location context.
- Logs must be accessible to support team for triage and diagnosis.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### AC1: REST Rate Request Includes Merchant Account Number
**Given** a merchant with a valid FedEx account number configured in app settings for a warehouse location  
**When** a rate request is triggered via REST API (e.g., customer at checkout)  
**Then** the REST API payload includes the merchant's `accountNumber` field in the rate request body  
**And** the account number matches the account configured for the selected warehouse location  

*Source: Card request; FedEx REST API docs; PluginHive app behaviour (multiple warehouse support)*

---

### AC2: Negotiated Rate Returned with SOAP vs REST Parity
**Given** a merchant with a FedEx account that has active negotiated rates configured  
**And** the app is using REST API (post-migration)  
**And** the merchant's account number is correctly passed in the rate request  
**And** a baseline SOAP rate exists for the same shipment configuration  
**When** a customer requests shipping rates at checkout for a domestic shipment  
**Then** the rate returned via REST matches the SOAP baseline rate within $0.50  
**And** this parity is confirmed for at least 3 service types (Ground, 2-Day, Priority Overnight)  
**And** the rate does NOT match the retail list rate for the same service  

*Source: Card request; Zendesk #383117; FedEx REST API docs; PluginHive rate comparison logging (T003)*

---

### AC3: Retail List Rate NOT Returned When Contract Pricing Exists
**Given** a merchant with an active FedEx account and negotiated rates  
**And** the REST API call is correctly formed with the merchant's account number  
**When** a rate request is processed  
**Then** the app does NOT return the retail/list rate  
**And** if a retail rate is returned despite correct account number, a diagnostic log entry is created with:
  - Full REST API request payload (including account number, rateType, shipment details)
  - Full REST API response (rates returned, service types, surcharges)
  - Timestamp and merchant account identifier
  - Warehouse location used for the request  

*Source: Card request; FedEx REST API docs; PluginHive app behaviour*

---

### AC4: Rate Mismatch Diagnostic Log Created
**Given** a rate discrepancy of $1.00 or more between the REST API response and the SOAP baseline rate  
**When** the rate request is processed  
**Then** a diagnostic log entry is written with:
  - Full REST API request payload (including account number, rateType, shipment details)
  - Full REST API response (rates returned, service types, surcharges)
  - SOAP baseline rate (retrieved from test data or prior SOAP response)
  - Mismatch amount and percentage difference
  - Timestamp and merchant account identifier
  - Warehouse location used for the request  
**And** the log is accessible to support team for triage  

*Source: Card request; Zendesk #383117; PluginHive rate comparison logging (T003)*

---

### AC5: No Regression to SOAP Users
**Given** a merchant still using SOAP API (not yet migrated)  
**When** a rate request is processed via SOAP  
**Then** the rate returned matches the negotiated contract rate (baseline behaviour before REST migration)  
**And** no change to SOAP rate logic is introduced by this fix  

*Source: Card request; PluginHive app behaviour (SOAP still supported)*

---

### AC6: Multiple Warehouse Account Isolation
**Given** a merchant with multiple warehouse locations, each with a different FedEx account number  
**When** a rate request is triggered for a shipment from Warehouse A  
**Then** the REST API call uses the account number for Warehouse A (not Warehouse B or default account)  
**And** the rate returned reflects Warehouse A's negotiated rates  

*Source: PluginHive docs (multiple warehouse support); Card request*

---

### AC7: Edge Case — Retail Account (No Contract Pricing)
**Given** a merchant with a valid FedEx account but NO negotiated rates (retail account)  
**When** a rate request is processed  
**Then** the app returns the retail list rate  
**And** no diagnostic log is created (this is expected behaviour)  

*Source: FedEx REST API docs; PluginHive app behaviour*

---

### AC8: Edge Case — Missing or Invalid Account Number
**Given** a merchant's FedEx account number is missing or invalid in app settings  
**When** a rate request is triggered  
**Then** the REST API call fails gracefully  
**And** the app returns the configured fallback rate for the applicable zone (Domestic, International, or Freight)  
**And** a diagnostic log entry is created with:
  - Error message (missing or invalid account number)
  - Fallback rate applied
  - Timestamp and merchant account identifier
  - Warehouse location used for the request  
**And** the customer sees the fallback rate at checkout  

*Source: Card request; PluginHive fallback rate docs*

---

## Priority
**High**

issues: AC2 and AC9 overlap significantly — both test SOAP vs REST rate parity within $0.50. AC9 is redundant.
AC4 references 'expected rate (from SOAP baseline or merchant contract documentation)' but AC is not testable without defining how/where baseline is retrieved or stored.

AC3 condition 'if a retail rate is returned, a diagnostic log entry is created' is vague — does this mean the app should detect and log retail rates automatically, or only when explicitly compared? Not testable as written.
AC8 fallback behaviour ('customer sees fallback rates or an error message per existing fallback logic') defers to unspecified existing logic — missing concrete expected result.
Missing prerequisite: How is the 'expected negotiated rate' determined for AC2, AC3, AC4, AC9? Is there a test data setup step (e.g., FedEx account with known contract rates configured in app)?
AC5 (SOAP regression) is not customer-impact coverage for the REST bug — it's a baseline preservation check. Should be separated or reframed as 'no regression to SOAP users'.
AC7 and AC8 are edge cases but lack clear trigger/setup: How does QA confirm a merchant account is 'retail only' vs 'has negotiated rates'? Missing test data or account setup assumption.
AC4 diagnostic log requirement references T003 (parallel work) but AC is written as if logging already exists. Unclear if this AC is blocked on T003 or if basic logging must be implemented here.

Missing scenario: What happens if REST API returns a rate but account number was NOT included in payload? (i.e., FedEx API accepted request without account number and returned retail rate). This is the likely root cause per 'Known Unknowns' but no AC directly tests the fix.
AC1 passes if 'accountNumber field is in payload' but does not verify the field value is correct or that FedEx API accepted it. Weak testability.
$0.50 tolerance in AC2 and AC9 is not justified by research context — no FedEx or PluginHive source provided for this threshold.
rewrite_instructions: Merge AC2 and AC9 into a single 'Rate Parity' scenario: test SOAP baseline vs REST with correct account number for 3+ service types, within $0.50 tolerance (with source justification for tolerance).

Rewrite AC4 to specify: (a) how 'expected rate' is determined (e.g., from FedEx Ship Manager, merchant contract, or SOAP baseline), (b) concrete log format/location, (c) trigger threshold ($1.00 mismatch) is testable.
Rewrite AC3 to clarify: Does the app actively detect and reject retail rates when contract rates exist, or only log them? Define the expected behaviour (reject vs. log vs. both).
Rewrite AC8 fallback behaviour: Replace 'per existing fallback logic' with concrete expected result (e.g., 'app returns configured fallback rate for Domestic/International/Freight as applicable').
Add prerequisite section: Define test data setup (e.g., 'FedEx test account with negotiated Ground rates configured in app; account number stored in warehouse location settings').
Separate AC5 into a distinct 'Regression' section (not an AC) or retitle as 'AC5: No Regression to SOAP Users — SOAP rate logic unchanged'.
Add test data assumption for AC7 and AC8: 'Test account types: (1) Retail account with no contract rates, (2) Account with missing/invalid account number in app settings'.
Clarify AC4 dependency on T003: If logging does not exist, add AC0 'Implement diagnostic logging for rate requests' or defer AC4 to post-T003.

Add new AC: 'AC1b: REST API Request Fails Gracefully if Account Number Omitted — When account number is missing from payload, REST API returns error or retail rate; app logs this as diagnostic event.'
Add source/justification for $0.50 tolerance threshold in AC2/AC9, or replace with 'exact match' if contract rates are deterministic.

69ea72440da24ff75870f37a.ac_text:
# FDX-175: Fix Product Weight Sync — Restore Queue Worker Update

## User Story
As a **Shopify store owner**, I want **product weight changes in Shopify to automatically sync to the FedEx app's database**, so that **shipping rates and labels are calculated with current product weights without manual correction**.

---

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

### PluginHive / App Behaviour
- **Product weight source of truth**: Shopify product weight (in Shipping tab) must be synced to MongoDB product document via the `products/update` webhook handler.
- **Weight sync path**: Shopify fires `products/update` webhook → app queues job → queue worker must call `updateProductFromImports` to write `weight` and `weightUnit` to DB.
- **Current broken state**: `updateProductFromImports` is commented out in the queue worker for `resource: PRODUCT, type: update` events; Shopify weight changes are queued but never persisted to MongoDB.
- **Downstream impact**: Order enhancer reads product weight via `findOneProductWithLean` from MongoDB. Stale DB weight causes incorrect volumetric calculations, wrong shipping quotes, and incorrect label generation.
- **Required fields for sync**: `weight` and `weightUnit` must be included in the field set passed to `updateProductFromImports`.

### FedEx / Shipping Constraints
- **Volumetric weight calculation**: App calculates per-product volumetric weight based on dimensions and weight stored in DB (see PluginHive v1.1.5 release notes, 23-01-2020).
- **Freight services**: FedEx Freight requires weight ≥ 68 kg; signature options are critical for freight. Stale weight may cause incorrect service selection.

### Prerequisites
- Queue worker service must be running and configured to process `products/update` webhooks from Shopify.
- A valid REST store and label-generation-capable order/setup are required.
- Product must exist in both Shopify and MongoDB before weight update is tested.

### Known Risks
- **Regression risk**: Product variant updates and product creation paths must not be broken by queue worker changes.
- **MCSL fix pattern**: Solution must follow the proven MCSL fix pattern; if `updateProductFromImports` was commented out due to a known issue, that issue must be investigated before uncommenting.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: Happy Path — Shopify Weight Update Syncs to DB and Affects Rate Quote
**Given** a product exists in Shopify with weight = 2 lb and is already synced to the app's MongoDB.  
**When** the store owner updates the product weight in Shopify (Shipping tab) to 3 lb.  
**Then** Shopify fires a `products/update` webhook → the app queues a job with `resource: PRODUCT, type: update` → the queue worker processes the job and calls `updateProductFromImports` → the product document in MongoDB is updated with `weight: 3` and `weightUnit: lb` → a subsequent rate quote for an order containing this product returns accurate shipping rates based on 3 lb weight → a label generated for this product displays the correct 3 lb weight.

**Expected Outcome**: Weight is persisted to MongoDB and used in downstream rate and label calculations.

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive app behaviour (product sync path); Code analysis (queue worker gap).

---

### Scenario 2: Weight Unit Sync — Metric and Imperial Units
**Given** a product exists in Shopify with weight = 1.5 kg (metric store).  
**When** the store owner updates the product weight to 2 kg in Shopify.  
**Then** the queue worker syncs both `weight: 2` and `weightUnit: kg` to MongoDB → a subsequent rate quote uses 2 kg (not converted or lost) → the label weight field displays 2 kg.

**Expected Outcome**: Both weight value and unit are persisted and used correctly in rate and label generation.

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive docs (weight and dimensions required, units must match shipper location); App behaviour (weight and weightUnit fields).

---

### Scenario 3: Regression — Product Variant Update Not Broken
**Given** a product with variants exists in Shopify and is synced to the app.  
**When** the store owner updates a variant's weight in Shopify.  
**Then** the queue worker processes the `products/update` webhook → variant weight is synced to MongoDB → the product's variant list in the app reflects the updated weight → no error or data loss occurs.

**Expected Outcome**: Variant weight updates are processed without breaking the update path.

**Source**: Card request; Regression watch (variant update path must not be broken).

---

### Scenario 4: Regression — Product Creation Path Unchanged
**Given** a new product is created in Shopify with weight = 1 lb.  
**When** Shopify fires a `products/create` webhook.  
**Then** the app queues a job with `resource: PRODUCT, type: create` → the queue worker processes the job → the product is inserted into MongoDB with `weight: 1` and `weightUnit: lb` → the product is available for rate quotes and label generation.

**Expected Outcome**: Product creation path continues to function without regression.

**Source**: Card request; Regression watch (product creation path must not be affected by queue worker changes).

---

### Scenario 5: Error Handling — Queue Worker Failure Logging
**Given** a product weight update is queued for processing.  
**When** the queue worker encounters an error while calling `updateProductFromImports` (e.g., DB connection failure, validation error).  
**Then** the error is logged to app logs with context (shop ID, product ID, attempted weight, error message) → the job is retried according to the queue's retry policy → if max retries are exceeded, the job is moved to a dead-letter queue or flagged for manual review.

**Expected Outcome**: Errors are logged with sufficient context for debugging; retry and dead-letter queue behaviour follows the app's documented queue policy.

**Source**: Card request; Queue worker error handling best practice.

---

## Priority
**High** — Product weight sync is a critical path for accurate shipping rate calculation and label generation; stale weights directly cause incorrect quotes and customer-facing shipping errors.

---

## Scenario Source Attribution

| Scenario | Source |
|----------|--------|
| Scenario 1 (Happy Path + Customer Impact) | Card request; PluginHive app behaviour; Code analysis |
| Scenario 2 (Weight Unit Sync) | Card request; PluginHive docs (weight/dimensions/units); App behaviour |
| Scenario 3 (Variant Regression) | Card request; Regression watch (variant update path) |
| Scenario 4 (Creation Regression) | Card request; Regression watch (product creation path) |
| Scenario 5 (Error Handling) | Card request; Queue worker error handling best practice |

---

## References
- [Trello Card](https://trello.com/c/xtBhV65m)
- [PluginHive Product Page](https://www.pluginhive.com/product/shopify-fedex-shipping-app-with-print-label-tracking/)
- [PluginHive FedEx Freight Rate Issues FAQ](https://www.pluginhive.com/fedex-freight-rate-issues-on-shopify-faqs/)
- [PluginHive Setup Guide](https://www.pluginhive.com/set-up-shopify-fedex-rates-labels-tracking-app/)
- FedEx REST API: `/ship/v1/shipments` (label generation); `/rate/v1/rates/quotes` (rate quotes)

issues: Scenario 6 and 7 are duplicative — they describe the same broken/fixed state from opposite angles; only one is needed as a before/after pair or acceptance criterion.
Scenario 8 (Error State / Queue Worker Failure Handling) is inferred and not grounded in the brief, card, or research context; no evidence that error handling is in scope for this bug fix.

Scenario 5 (Shopify Duplication / Commune Design) references a specific test case ('Studio Ford napkin') that is not documented in the provided research context or customer issue summary; the claim that duplication causes weight inheritance issues is unsupported.

Missing prerequisite: No explicit statement that the queue worker must be deployed and running for webhooks to be processed; unclear if this is a code-only fix or requires infrastructure/deployment validation.

Scenario 3 (Variant Update) assumes variant weight sync is part of the `products/update` webhook scope, but the brief and PluginHive docs do not explicitly confirm variant weight is included in the sync payload or handled by `updateProductFromImports`.
Scenario 4 (Product Creation) is a regression watch but not directly related to the bug fix (commented-out code in update path); it may be out of scope unless the fix touches the create path.

Missing customer-impact coverage: No scenario validates that the fix resolves the actual customer issue (incorrect shipping quotes or label generation due to stale weight); Scenario 1 and 7 imply this but do not explicitly test downstream impact (rate quote accuracy, label weight field).
Vague expected result in Scenario 8: 'notified (if applicable)' is not testable; no acceptance criterion defines when/how the store owner is notified or what the notification contains.
rewrite_instructions: Merge Scenario 6 and 7 into a single before/after scenario or remove Scenario 6 (broken behaviour) and keep only Scenario 7 (corrected behaviour) as the acceptance criterion.

Remove Scenario 8 (Error State / Queue Worker Failure Handling) unless error handling is explicitly required in the card or brief; if in scope, ground it in documented retry policy and dead-letter queue behaviour.

Remove or replace Scenario 5 (Duplication / Commune Design) with a generic variant/duplication regression test, or provide explicit evidence from the brief/research that Shopify duplication is a known issue for this product.
Add a prerequisite: 'Queue worker service must be running and configured to process `products/update` webhooks from Shopify.'

Clarify Scenario 3 (Variant Update): Confirm whether variant weight is included in the `products/update` webhook payload and whether `updateProductFromImports` handles variant weight updates; if not, remove or mark as out of scope.
Move Scenario 4 (Product Creation) to a separate regression test suite or remove it if the fix only touches the update path; if kept, justify why creation path must be re-tested.

Add a new scenario: 'Customer-Impact Validation — Stale Weight Does Not Affect Rate Quote' — verify that after the fix, a rate quote for an order containing the updated product uses the correct weight and returns accurate shipping rates.

Replace vague language in Scenario 8 ('notified if applicable') with a concrete, testable criterion (e.g., 'error is logged to app logs with shop ID, product ID, and error message') or remove the notification requirement.

69ea7245e6879034b5bf2e6b.card_name: From SL: FDX-164 — Fix importerOfRecord typo on commercial invoice [#385278]
69ea7245e6879034b5bf2e6b.ac_text:
# FDX-164: Fix importerOfRecord Typo on Commercial Invoice

## User Story
As a merchant using FedEx Importer of Record (IOR) functionality, I want the IOR details to appear correctly on my commercial invoices, so that customs clearance and invoice accuracy are maintained for international shipments.

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

### FedEx REST API Requirements
- **Base URLs**: Production `https://apis.fedex.com` | Sandbox `https://apis-sandbox.fedex.com`
- **API Migration**: App has fully migrated from SOAP to REST; SOAP structures are no longer used.
- **Silent Field Rejection**: FedEx REST API silently ignores unrecognised field names in request payloads — no error is returned when a field key is misspelled. This means incorrect field names fail silently without alerting the merchant or developer.

### PluginHive IOR Implementation
- **IOR Feature Guard**: IOR details are only included in the request payload when `isImporterOfRecordEnabled` is `true` on the Additional Account configuration.
- **Field Name Requirement**: The correct FedEx REST field name is `importerOfRecord` (not `importedOfRecord`).
- **Scope**: IOR configuration applies only to merchants with an Additional Account set up; Primary Account labels (where `isImporterOfRecordEnabled: false`) are unaffected.
- **Commercial Invoice**: IOR name, address, and account number must appear on the generated commercial invoice PDF for international shipments.

### Known Constraints
- Non-English characters in addresses are not recognised by FedEx; all addresses must use English characters only.
- IOR is an optional feature and only active when explicitly enabled in Additional Account settings.

## Prerequisites

### Account & Configuration Setup
- **Additional Account with IOR enabled**: Test account must have `isImporterOfRecordEnabled: true` and merchant details (name, address, account number) fully populated in Additional Account settings.
- **Test Shipper Address**: Must contain English characters only; no special characters or non-Latin scripts.
- **Test Store Access**: Access to `support-qa-collab-releasetest.myshopify.com` or equivalent REST-capable test store with label-generation permissions.

### Test Data
- **International Test Order**: Order with destination address in a country requiring customs documentation (e.g., order 1222 on support-qa store).
- **Product Configuration**: Test products must have weight and dimensions configured in Shopify product shipping tab and app Products section.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: IOR Field Name Corrected in REST Request Payload
**Given** a merchant has IOR enabled on an Additional Account with merchant details populated  
**When** a label is generated for an international order via the FedEx REST API  
**Then** the request payload contains the field key `importerOfRecord` (not `importedOfRecord`)  
**And** the payload structure is valid JSON  
**And** the FedEx API returns HTTP 200 or 201 (not a field-rejection error)  
→ *Source: Code analysis (fedexRestRequestBuilder.js:680); FedEx REST API silent rejection behaviour*

### Scenario 2: IOR Details Appear on Commercial Invoice PDF
**Given** a merchant has IOR enabled on an Additional Account with merchant details populated  
**And** a label has been generated with the corrected `importerOfRecord` field  
**When** the commercial invoice PDF is generated and downloaded  
**Then** the IOR name, address, and account number are visually present on the invoice  
**And** the values match the Additional Account configuration  
→ *Source: L3 QA reproduction (support-qa-collab-releasetest.myshopify.com, order 1222); PluginHive IOR feature behaviour*

### Scenario 3: Primary Account Labels Remain Unaffected (Regression)
**Given** a merchant is using an Additional Account with `isImporterOfRecordEnabled: false` (Primary Account mode)  
**When** a label is generated for an international order  
**Then** the label generation succeeds  
**And** no `importerOfRecord` or `importedOfRecord` field is included in the request payload  
**And** the commercial invoice does not include IOR details  
→ *Source: Code analysis (IOR guard at line 679); PluginHive Primary Account behaviour*

### Scenario 4: IOR Enabled but Merchant Details Incomplete (Edge Case)
**Given** IOR is enabled on an Additional Account but merchant name, address, or account number are blank or missing  
**When** a label is generated for an international order  
**Then** the request payload includes the `importerOfRecord` field with null or empty values  
**And** the FedEx API returns a validation error (HTTP 4xx)  
**And** the label generation fails gracefully with a user-facing error message indicating missing IOR details  
→ *Source: Edge case validation; PluginHive IOR configuration requirements*

### Scenario 5: Customer-Impact Validation — IOR Field Rejection Resolved
**Given** a merchant previously experienced silent IOR field rejection (invoices blocked for customs clearance)  
**When** the fix is deployed and a label is generated with IOR enabled and merchant details populated  
**Then** the commercial invoice now includes IOR details  
**And** the merchant can proceed with customs clearance without manual workarounds  
→ *Source: Customer issue resolution (Ticket #385278); PluginHive IOR feature behaviour*

## Priority
**High** — IOR is a critical feature for international merchants; silent field rejection means invoices are currently invalid for customs clearance, blocking international shipments for affected accounts.

## Scenario Source Attribution
- Scenario 1 → Code analysis (fedexRestRequestBuilder.js:680); FedEx REST API silent field rejection behaviour
- Scenario 2 → L3 QA reproduction (support-qa-collab-releasetest.myshopify.com, order 1222); PluginHive IOR feature behaviour
- Scenario 3 → Code analysis (IOR guard at line 679); PluginHive Primary Account behaviour
- Scenario 4 → Edge case validation; PluginHive IOR configuration requirements
- Scenario 5 → Customer issue resolution (Ticket #385278); PluginHive IOR feature behaviour

## Test Scope

### Automation Coverage Required
- **Unit Tests**: Confirm `importerOfRecord` key is present in REST request payload when `isImporterOfRecordEnabled: true` and merchant details are populated
- **Integration Tests**: Label generation with IOR-enabled Additional Account; verify payload structure and FedEx API response code
- **E2E / QA Manual**: Commercial invoice PDF generation and visual verification of IOR details on invoice (order 1222, support-qa store)

### App Sections Affected
- **Label Generation** (REST API request builder)
- **Commercial Invoice** (PDF rendering with IOR details)
- **Additional Account Settings** (IOR configuration guard and merchant details validation)

### Existing Test Areas to Verify
- Single Label generation (IOR-enabled account with complete merchant details)
- International Label generation (IOR-enabled account)
- Return Labels (if IOR applies)
- Bulk Orders (if IOR applies)

### Files to Audit
- `server/shared/adaptors/fedexRest/fedexRestRequestBuilder.js` (line 680 — primary fix location)
- `server/shared/adaptors/fedexRest/fedexRestRequestBuilder.js` (entire `getCustomsClearanceDetailFor` method — audit for similar typos)

## Out of Scope
- Mobile / responsive / viewport testing (web/desktop only)
- Changes to SOAP request builder (app has fully migrated to REST)
- Changes to Primary Account label generation (IOR disabled)
- Changes to FedEx API contract or field definitions
- Merchant UI/UX for IOR configuration (settings already exist)
- Bulk delete or label management features

- [FedEx REST API Production](https://apis.fedex.com)
- [FedEx REST API Sandbox](https://apis-sandbox.fedex.com)
- [PluginHive FedEx Customs Documentation FAQs](https://www.pluginhive.com/fedex-customs-documentation-in-shopify-faqs/)
- [PluginHive FedEx Shipping App Product Page](https://www.pluginhive.com/product/shopify-fedex-shipping-app-with-print-label-tracking/)

# FDX-112: Restrict soldTo to International Shipments Only

## User Story
As a **developer / QA engineer**, I want **the FedEx REST request builder to exclude the `soldTo` field for domestic shipments**, so that **domestic labels do not fail validation due to incomplete or short billing address fields that FedEx rejects when `soldTo` is present**.

---

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

1. **FedEx API Behaviour — soldTo Field**
   - `soldTo` (billing party details) is **required for international shipments** to establish duty/tax responsibility and customs clearance.
   - For **domestic same-country shipments** (e.g. US→US), `soldTo` is **not required** and may cause validation failures if billing address fields are incomplete or below FedEx's minimum length thresholds (e.g. city field too short).
   - FedEx error: `CITY.TOO.SHORT` or similar validation errors occur when `soldTo` is populated with incomplete billing data on domestic requests.

2. **PluginHive App Behaviour — International Shipment Detection**
   - The app already defines `isInternationalShipment(shipFromCountry, shipToCountry)` helper at `fedexRestRequestBuilder.js:44`.
   - Logic: `isInternational = (from !== to)` — shipment is international if origin and destination countries differ.
   - Other international-only fields (e.g. `dutiesPayment` at line 702) already use this guard; `soldTo` at line 2499 does not.

3. **Current Implementation Gap**
   - Line 2499 in `fedexRestRequestBuilder.js` unconditionally assigns `returnObject.soldTo = this.getSoldToDetailsUsing(order, preferredCarrier)` for all shipments.
   - This causes domestic requests to include `soldTo` with potentially incomplete billing data, triggering FedEx validation errors.

4. **Merge Coordination Risk**
   - FDX-111 also modifies the `soldTo` code path in the same file; coordinate merge order and regression testing.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: Domestic Shipment — soldTo Excluded
**Given** a domestic shipment (origin country = destination country, e.g. US→US)  
**And** the order has a billing address with a short city name (e.g. "NY")  
**When** the FedEx REST request builder constructs the shipment request  
**Then** the request body does **not** include a `soldTo` node  
**And** the request succeeds without `CITY.TOO.SHORT` or similar validation errors  

**Source**: Card request; FedEx API behaviour; Code analysis (line 2499 unconditional assignment)

---

### Scenario 2: International Shipment — soldTo Included (Regression)
**Given** an international shipment (origin country ≠ destination country, e.g. US→UK)  
**And** the order has a valid billing address with all required fields  
**When** the FedEx REST request builder constructs the shipment request  
**Then** the request body **includes** a `soldTo` node with billing party details  
**And** the request succeeds with FedEx (no validation errors)  

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive docs (international shipments require billing details); Code analysis (existing pattern for `dutiesPayment`)

---

### Scenario 3: DDP International Shipment — soldTo Present (Regression)
**Given** an international shipment with Duties/Taxes Paid (DDP) duty payment option  
**And** the order has a valid billing address  
**When** the FedEx REST request builder constructs the shipment request  
**Then** the request body **includes** a `soldTo` node  
**And** the `dutiesPayment` field is also present  
**And** the request succeeds with FedEx  

**Source**: Card request; Code analysis (dutiesPayment at line 702 already checks isInternational; soldTo must follow same pattern)

---

### Scenario 4: Third-Party Billing International Shipment — soldTo Included (Regression)
**Given** an international shipment with third-party billing enabled  
**And** the order has a valid billing address for the third party  
**When** the FedEx REST request builder constructs the shipment request  
**Then** the request body **includes** a `soldTo` node with third-party billing details  
**And** the request succeeds with FedEx  

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive docs (international shipments with billing options); Code analysis (getSoldToDetailsUsing handles third-party logic)

---

### Scenario 5: Domestic Shipment with Complete Billing Address — soldTo Still Excluded
**Given** a domestic shipment (US→US)  
**And** the order has a **complete** billing address with all fields populated  
**When** the FedEx REST request builder constructs the shipment request  
**Then** the request body does **not** include a `soldTo` node  
**And** the request succeeds (confirms soldTo exclusion is not conditional on address completeness)  

**Source**: Card request; Code analysis (guard should be on isInternational only, not address validation)

---

## Priority
**High** — Domestic shipments are currently failing FedEx validation due to unconditional `soldTo` inclusion with incomplete billing data; this blocks a core use case (domestic labels) and is a regression from expected FedEx API behaviour.

---

## Scenario Source Attribution

- **Scenario 1** → Card request; FedEx API behaviour; Code analysis (fedexRestRequestBuilder.js:2499)
- **Scenario 2** → Card request; PluginHive docs; Code analysis (isInternationalShipment helper at line 44)
- **Scenario 3** → Card request; Code analysis (dutiesPayment pattern at line 702)
- **Scenario 4** → Card request; PluginHive docs (third-party billing); Code analysis (getSoldToDetailsUsing method)
- **Scenario 5** → Card request; Code analysis (guard logic should isolate on isInternational, not address fields)

---

## Test Scope

### Unit Tests
- **File**: `server/shared/adaptors/fedexRest/__tests__/fedexRestRequestBuilder.test.js`
- **Coverage**:
  - Domestic shipment request body: assert `soldTo` key is absent
  - International shipment request body: assert `soldTo` key is present with valid billing details
  - Verify `isInternationalShipment` helper is called and used to gate `soldTo` assignment

### Integration Tests
- **File**: `__tests__/integration/label.domestic.test.js` (existing domestic label flow)
- **Coverage**:
  - Generate domestic label (US→US) with short billing city; verify label succeeds and no `CITY.TOO.SHORT` error
  - Verify label prints and barcode is scannable

- **File**: `__tests__/integration/label.international.test.js` (existing international label flow)
- **Coverage**:
  - Generate international label (US→UK) with valid billing address; verify `soldTo` is present in request
  - Verify label succeeds and customs details are processed
  - Generate DDP international label; verify `soldTo` and `dutiesPayment` both present
  - Generate third-party billing international label; verify `soldTo` contains third-party details

### Regression Tests
- **File**: `__tests__/regression/soldTo.regression.test.js` (new)
- **Coverage**:
  - Domestic label with various billing address formats (short city, abbreviated state, etc.); all should succeed without `soldTo`
  - International label with DDP, DDU, third-party billing; all should include `soldTo` and succeed
  - Verify no change to `getSoldToDetailsUsing` logic — only the guard is added

### Affected Test Areas
- **Single Label** (domestic and international flows)
- **Label Domestic** (core regression area)
- **Label International** (core regression area)
- **Settings** (if billing address configuration is tested)

---

## Out of Scope

- Mobile / responsive / viewport testing (web/desktop only)
- Changes to `getSoldToDetailsUsing` method logic or return structure
- Changes to billing address validation rules (only the conditional guard is added)
- Changes to FedEx API error handling or retry logic
- Changes to customs documentation or commodity detail handling
- UI/UX for billing address entry or display

# FDX-113: Surface Accurate Error Codes on Label Failure

## User Story
As a **merchant**, I want **FedEx error codes (e.g. `CITY.TOO.SHORT`, `STREETLINE1.EMPTY`) to be displayed in the label failure UI**, so that **I can quickly diagnose and fix shipping configuration issues without contacting support**.

---

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

1. **FedEx Error Code Format**  
   FedEx REST API returns error codes in dot-notation (e.g. `CITY.TOO.SHORT`, `STREETLINE1.EMPTY`). These are distinct from HTTP status codes and must be captured from the error response body.

2. **Error Object Capture**  
   The app's `getParsedErrorsFrom()` function in `fedexRestShipmentHelper.js` correctly extracts `errorCode`, `errorMessage`, `resolution`, and `carrierErrorMessage` from FedEx responses. This extraction is working as designed.

3. **Package Document Persistence**  
   The `errorObject` (containing `errorCode`, `errorMessage`, `resolution`, and `carrierErrorMessage`) must be persisted to the MongoDB package document when a label fails. The persistence must occur in `fedexRestShipmentHelper.js` via `updatePackageDocument()` before the failure response is returned to the API caller.

4. **UI Display Layer**  
   `LabelErrorInfo.js` already contains the logic to render `packages[0].errorObject.errorCode` as "FedEx Error Code". No UI changes are required if the data is persisted correctly.

5. **Mapped vs. Unmapped Errors**  
   Some error codes (e.g. authentication failures) are mapped to user-friendly messages via `additionalInfoMapper`. Unmapped codes (e.g. `CITY.TOO.SHORT`) should display the raw FedEx error code and message. Both the raw `errorCode` and user-friendly message must coexist in the persisted data and UI.

6. **Order Status Requirement**  
   Labels can only be generated for orders in "Unfulfilled" status. Failed label attempts must occur on valid unfulfilled orders.

---

## Prerequisites

### Test Data Setup
Before executing any scenario, the following test orders must be created in an unfulfilled state:
- **Order A**: Shipping address missing city (triggers `CITY.TOO.SHORT`)
- **Order B**: Shipping address missing street line 1 (triggers `STREETLINE1.EMPTY`)
- **Order C**: Shipping address missing both city AND street line 1 (triggers multiple errors)
- **Order D**: Valid shipping address with valid FedEx account credentials (for regression testing)
- **Order E**: Invalid FedEx account credentials (for mapped error testing)

### System Requirements
- Valid REST store with FedEx account configured
- FedEx account must have label-generation capability enabled
- MongoDB access to inspect package documents
- Packages API endpoint accessible and returning full `errorObject` in response

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: Unmapped FedEx Error Code Persists and Displays
**Given** an unfulfilled order (Order A) with a shipping address missing city information  
**When** a label is generated and FedEx returns error code `CITY.TOO.SHORT`  
**Then**  
- The package document in MongoDB includes `errorObject: { errorCode: 'CITY.TOO.SHORT', errorMessage: '...', resolution: '...', carrierErrorMessage: '...' }`  
- The packages API endpoint returns the `errorObject` field in the response with all four fields intact  
- The LabelErrorInfo UI displays "FedEx Error Code: CITY.TOO.SHORT" (not `--` or blank)  
- The raw FedEx error message is also visible in the UI  
- The Shopify order ID is present as CUSTOMER_REFERENCE in the failed label request  

**Source**: Card request + AI code analysis (fedexRestShipmentHelper.js:783–792 builds errorObject; LabelErrorInfo.js already renders it); PluginHive FAQ #1122 (service activation troubleshooting)

---

### Scenario 2: Multiple Unmapped Error Codes Display Correctly
**Given** an unfulfilled order (Order C) with multiple address validation failures (missing street line 1 AND missing city)  
**When** a label is generated and FedEx returns error codes `STREETLINE1.EMPTY` and `CITY.TOO.SHORT`  
**Then**  
- The package document captures the primary error code in `errorObject.errorCode` (or all codes if the API supports an array)  
- The UI displays the primary error code with its corresponding message  
- No error codes are lost or overwritten during persistence  
- The `errorObject` field is not null or undefined  

**Source**: Card request; inferred from multi-error handling in getParsedErrorsFrom()

---

### Scenario 3: Mapped Error Codes Still Display User-Friendly Messages (Regression)
**Given** an unfulfilled order (Order E) with invalid FedEx account credentials  
**When** a label is generated and FedEx returns an authentication error (mapped in `additionalInfoMapper`)  
**Then**  
- The package document includes `errorObject` with the raw error code (e.g. `AUTHENTICATION_FAILED`)  
- The `errorObject.errorCode` field is captured in MongoDB alongside the user-friendly message  
- The UI displays the user-friendly message from `additionalInfoMapper` (not the raw code)  
- The "Additional Info" section in LabelErrorInfo still renders correctly  
- Both the raw `errorCode` and mapped message coexist without conflict  

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive FAQ #1122 (service activation and configuration verification); existing app behaviour

---

### Scenario 4: Successful Labels Are Not Affected (Regression)
**Given** an unfulfilled order (Order D) with valid shipping address and FedEx account  
**When** a label is generated successfully  
**Then**  
- The package document does NOT include an `errorObject` field, or `errorObject` is null/empty  
- The label displays normally with tracking number and status "OC" (Order Created)  
- No error message appears in the UI  
- The packages API response does not include an `errorObject` field for successful labels  

**Source**: Card request; existing app behaviour

---

### Scenario 5: Error Object Persists Across Page Reload
**Given** a label failure with error code `CITY.TOO.SHORT` has been saved to the package document (Order A)  
**When** the user navigates away and returns to the order detail page  
**Then**  
- The error code is still displayed in the UI  
- The MongoDB package document still contains `errorObject` with the same `errorCode` value  
- The `errorObject` is not lost due to session/cache expiry  

**Source**: Card request; inferred from persistence requirement

---

### Scenario 6: Error Object Extraction and Persistence (Root Cause Verification)
**Given** a label generation fails with FedEx error code `CITY.TOO.SHORT` (Order A)  
**When** the app receives the FedEx error response  
**Then**  
- The `getParsedErrorsFrom()` function extracts the `errorObject` from the FedEx response  
- The `fedexRestShipmentHelper.js` calls `updatePackageDocument()` with the `errorObject` before returning the failure response  
- The package document is updated in MongoDB with the `errorObject` field  
- The API response includes the persisted `errorObject` for the UI to render  

**Source**: Card request; AI code analysis (fedexRestShipmentHelper.js persistence path); review findings (root cause clarification)

---

## Priority
**HIGH** — Merchants cannot diagnose FedEx validation errors without error codes, leading to support escalations and delayed shipments. The UI layer is ready; only the data persistence path needs verification and potential fix.

---

## Scenario Source Attribution

- **Scenario 1** → Card request (#FDX-113); AI code analysis (fedexRestShipmentHelper.js:783–792, LabelErrorInfo.js); PluginHive FAQ #1122 (service activation troubleshooting)
- **Scenario 2** → Card request; inferred from getParsedErrorsFrom() multi-error handling; review findings (specific error codes required)
- **Scenario 3** → Card request; PluginHive FAQ #1122; existing app behaviour (additionalInfoMapper); review findings (raw errorCode + mapped

# FDX-115: Wire SLGP Shipment Purpose Override to Label Request

## User Story
As a **merchant using the Shopify Label Generation Panel (SLGP)**, I want to **override the global "Purpose of Shipment" setting per label**, so that **international shipments with different purposes (Sample, Gift, Return, Personal Use) generate correct customs declarations without requiring global setting changes**.

---

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

### FedEx API Requirements
- **Shipment Purpose Field**: `shipmentPurpose` in FedEx REST API request; required for international shipments to populate customs declaration.
- **Valid Values**: `SOLD`, `GIFT`, `SAMPLE`, `RETURN`, `PERSONAL_USE` (per FedEx REST API spec).
- **Return Label Field**: `returnPurposeOfShipment` used for return shipments; separate from standard `shipmentPurpose`.
- **Customs Impact**: Purpose value directly affects commercial invoice and ETD (Electronic Trade Documents) generation; incorrect purpose may cause customs delays or rejections.
- **Default Fallback**: If no purpose is provided, FedEx defaults to `SOLD`.

### PluginHive App Behaviour
- **Global Setting**: Merchants configure default `Purpose of Shipment` in **App Settings → International Settings**.
- **Return Setting**: Merchants configure separate `Return Purpose of Shipment` in **App Settings → Return Settings**.
- **SLGP Override**: When generating a label via the Shopify Label Generation Panel, merchants can override the global setting via `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment` in the label POST request body.
- **Current Bug**: The label route handler does not merge the SLGP override into the request context before passing to the FedEx request builder, causing the override to be discarded and the global default to be used instead.

### Request Builder Resolution Chain
- **Priority Order** (line 497, `fedexRestRequestBuilder.js`):
  1. `purposeOfShipmentFromOrder` (highest priority; includes SLGP override when merged)
  2. `purposeOfShipmentFromPreferredCarrier` (fallback to saved carrier settings)
  3. `constants.SOLD` (hardcoded default)
- **Return Label Path**: Uses separate `returnPurposeOfShipment` logic (line 491); configured via App Settings → Return Settings; not affected by SLGP override.

### Known Limitations
- **Return Labels**: Return shipment purpose is handled via a separate code path and configuration; SLGP override does not apply to return labels.
- **Bulk Label Generation**: Bulk operations must also respect per-order SLGP overrides (scope TBD; confirm if bulk route uses same builder).
- **ETD Integration**: If ETD is enabled, the purpose value must propagate to ETD document generation (verify in acceptance testing).
- **Service Type Constraints**: FedEx may restrict certain purpose values to specific service types or shipment configurations; test with international orders only.

### Test Verification Method
- **FedEx Request Payload Verification**: All assertions about `shipmentPurpose` or `returnPurposeOfShipment` values must be verified via server logs (request builder invocation logs) or API request inspection tools.
- **Commercial Invoice Verification**: Purpose field in generated PDF must be extracted and compared to the FedEx request value (method: manual PDF inspection or programmatic PDF parsing).
- **ETD Document Verification**: When ETD is enabled, retrieve the ETD document via the FedEx tracking API or app label details page and inspect the purpose field.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: SLGP Override Replaces Global Default
**Given**
- Global `Purpose of Shipment` is set to `SOLD` in App Settings → International Settings.
- A merchant is generating a label for an international order via the SLGP.
- The merchant selects `SAMPLE` from the SLGP purpose dropdown.

**When**
- The merchant submits the label request with `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment = "SAMPLE"`.

**Then**
- Server logs confirm `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment = "SAMPLE"` was received at the label route.
- Server logs at the builder invocation confirm `purposeOfShipment = "SAMPLE"` was passed to the FedEx request builder.
- The FedEx REST API request includes `shipmentPurpose: "SAMPLE"`.
- The commercial invoice PDF generated by FedEx displays "Sample" as the purpose.

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive app behaviour; FedEx REST API spec.

---

### Scenario 2: Global Default Preserved When SLGP Not Changed
**Given**
- Global `Purpose of Shipment` is set to `GIFT` in App Settings → International Settings.
- A merchant is generating a label for an international order via the SLGP.
- The merchant does NOT change the purpose field in the SLGP (leaves it blank or unmodified).

**When**
- The merchant submits the label request without `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment` (or with `null`/`undefined`).

**Then**
- Server logs confirm `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment` was not provided or was null.
- Server logs at the builder invocation confirm `purposeOfShipment = "GIFT"` (from global settings) was passed to the builder.
- The FedEx REST API request includes `shipmentPurpose: "GIFT"`.
- The commercial invoice PDF displays "Gift" as the purpose.

**Source**: Card request; FedEx REST API spec; PluginHive app behaviour.

---

### Scenario 3: All Five Purpose Values Round-Trip Correctly
**Given**
- Global `Purpose of Shipment` is set to `SOLD`.
- A merchant generates five separate international labels, each with a different SLGP override: `GIFT`, `SAMPLE`, `RETURN`, `PERSONAL_USE`, `SOLD`.
- All labels are for international orders (e.g., US→Canada, US→EU) with FedEx Express or Ground service.

**When**
- Each label is submitted with the corresponding `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment` value.

**Then**
- Server logs confirm each value was received at the label route and passed to the builder without truncation or transformation.
- Each FedEx REST API request includes the correct `shipmentPurpose` value.
- All five commercial invoices display the correct purpose.

**Source**: Card request; FedEx REST API spec.

---

### Scenario 4: SLGP Override Does Not Affect Return Label Purpose Path
**Given**
- A return label is being generated for an order.
- The SLGP purpose field is set to `SAMPLE`.
- Global `Return Purpose of Shipment` is configured separately in App Settings → Return Settings (e.g., set to `RETURN`).

**When**
- The return label is submitted with `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment = "SAMPLE"`.

**Then**
- Server logs confirm the return label path was invoked (separate from standard label path).
- The FedEx REST API request uses `returnPurposeOfShipment: "RETURN"` from App Settings (not the SLGP override value).
- The return label purpose is NOT affected by the SLGP override.

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive app behaviour (return labels use separate code path at line 491).

---

### Scenario 5: ETD Integration — Purpose Propagates to Electronic Trade Documents
**Given**
- ETD (Electronic Trade Documents) is enabled in App Settings → International Settings.
- A merchant generates an international label with SLGP purpose override set to `SAMPLE`.

**When**
- The label is submitted with `customCarrierSpecificData.purposeOfShipment = "SAMPLE"`.

**Then**
- Server logs confirm `purposeOfShipment = "SAMPLE"` was passed to the FedEx request builder.
- The FedEx REST API request includes `shipmentPurpose: "SAMPLE"`.
- The FedEx REST API response includes an ETD document URL or reference.
- When the ETD document is retrieved (via FedEx tracking API or app label details page) and inspected, it displays the shipment purpose as "Sample".
- Commercial invoice and ETD are consistent.

**Source**: Card request; PluginHive docs (ETD eliminates need for paper customs documents); FedEx REST API spec.

---

This is circular logic.

Scenario 5 (ETD Integration) lacks a prerequisite: it assumes ETD is enabled and that FedEx will generate ETD documents, but does not specify how to verify ETD was actually generated or how to inspect its contents. The AC should specify the observable outcome (FedEx request includes correct purpose) without dictating the merge strategy.
Scenario 8 is incomplete: the text cuts off mid-sentence ('A merchant navigates to App Settings → International') and the expected result is missing entirely.

Missing prerequisite for all scenarios: no mention of how to verify the FedEx REST API request payload was actually sent with the correct shipmentPurpose value. Are server logs the only source of truth, or should the AC include API response inspection or label metadata verification?

Scenario 3 (All Five Values) does not specify which order types or shipment configurations are tested (domestic vs. international, different weight/dimension combinations). FedEx may reject certain purpose values for certain service types.

Scenario 7 (Regression — PDF Match) assumes the commercial invoice PDF is always generated and downloadable, but does not specify the download method or how to extract/verify the purpose field from the PDF programmatically.

Missing customer-impact regression: no AC verifies that merchants can still generate labels without SLGP override (backward compatibility) or that existing workflows are not broken by the merge logic.

Scenario 1 and 2 both reference 'Server logs confirm...' as the assertion method, but logs are not a reliable test artifact. Should include API request inspection or label metadata verification instead.

No AC covers error handling: what happens if an invalid purpose value is submitted in SLGP (e.g., typo or unsupported value)? Should the app reject it, fall back to global default, or pass it to FedEx and let FedEx reject it?

# FDX-111: Sanitize Empty Billing Address Nodes Before Label Request

## User Story
As a **merchant with an international FedEx shipment**, I want the app to **validate and sanitize billing address fields before sending them to FedEx**, so that **empty or invalid billing address values do not cause label generation failures**.

---

## Domain Rules / FedEx Constraints

### FedEx REST API Requirements
- **Street lines**: Must be non-empty strings; empty or whitespace-only lines must be filtered out before sending to FedEx
- **State/Province Code**: Optional; must be omitted if null or empty
- **Postal Code**: Optional; must be omitted if null or empty
- **City field**: Required if address is present; must be non-empty
- **SoldTo node**: If all address fields are empty or stripped, the entire `soldTo` node must be omitted from the request
- **Account number**: Required in all requests; must match FedEx billing account number (not API key)
- **Required headers**: Authorization (Bearer token), Content-Type (application/json), X-Customer-Transaction-Id (UUID for tracing)

### PluginHive / Shopify Constraints
- Shopify does not enforce billing address field completeness; merchants can save orders with partial or empty billing addresses
- The app cannot prevent invalid data at order creation time; validation must occur at label-generation time
- Canadian billing addresses are supported but must still meet FedEx field requirements
- Custom box sizes are supported for return labels; billing for return labels follows standard FedEx rates

### Known Prerequisites
- **International shipment detection**: This fix applies **only to international shipments** where `getSoldToDetailsUsing` is called
  - Trigger condition: shipment destination is outside US/CA, or international flag is explicitly set to true
- **Domestic shipment protection**: Domestic shipments are protected by FDX-112 (separate guard that prevents `getSoldToDetailsUsing` from being called)
- **Billing arrangement support**: DDP (Duties, Taxes, Fees Paid by Shipper), DTP (Duties, Taxes, Fees Paid by Recipient), and Third-Party billing flows must all receive valid `soldTo` data when present
- **Coordinate with FDX-112**: Avoid double-guarding in the same code path; FDX-112 handles domestic orders, FDX-111 handles international orders

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Scenario 1: International Label with Valid Billing Address (Regression)
**Given** an international order with a fully valid billing address (non-empty city, non-empty street, valid state/postal code)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app includes the complete `soldTo` node in the FedEx request with all valid fields  
**And** the label generates successfully  
**And** no fields are incorrectly stripped or omitted  

*Source: Card request; regression requirement*

---

### Scenario 2: International Label with Field-Level Sanitization
**Given** an international order with a billing address containing one or more empty/null fields (empty state, empty postal code, empty/whitespace street lines)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app applies the following sanitization rules:
  - Empty or whitespace-only street lines are filtered out; only non-empty street lines are included
  - Empty or null `stateOrProvinceCode` is omitted from the request
  - Empty or null `postalCode` is omitted from the request
  - City field (if present) is included if non-empty
**And** the FedEx request includes only the valid, non-empty fields  
**And** the label generates successfully  

*Source: Card request; AI code analysis; FedEx REST API constraints*

---

### Scenario 3: International Label with Completely Empty Billing Address
**Given** an international order with a completely empty or null billing address (all fields null/empty)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app omits the entire `soldTo` node from the FedEx request  
**And** the label generates successfully  
**And** FedEx processes the request without billing address information  

*Source: Card request; AI code analysis; FDX-112 coordination*

---

### Scenario 4: International Label with Empty Street Lines Only
**Given** an international order with a billing address containing empty or whitespace-only street lines but valid city, state, and postal code  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app filters out empty street lines before sending to FedEx  
**And** only non-empty street lines are included in the `addressLine` node  
**And** if all street lines are empty after filtering, the entire address line node is omitted  
**And** the label generates successfully  

*Source: Card request; AI code analysis; FedEx REST API constraints*

---

### Scenario 5: DDP Billing Flow with Sanitized Address (Regression)
**Given** an international order with DDP (Duties, Taxes, Fees Paid by Shipper) billing arrangement and a partially invalid billing address (e.g., empty postal code)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app sanitizes the billing address per Scenario 2 rules  
**And** the DDP billing arrangement XML is preserved in the request with correct Duties Payment fields  
**And** the label generates successfully  

*Source: Card request; PluginHive billing flows; PluginHive FAQ on Duties Payment XML*

---

### Scenario 6: DTP Billing Flow with Sanitized Address (Regression)
**Given** an international order with DTP (Duties, Taxes, Fees Paid by Recipient) billing arrangement and a partially invalid billing address (e.g., empty state code)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app sanitizes the billing address per Scenario 2 rules  
**And** the DTP billing arrangement XML is preserved in the request with correct Duties Payment fields  
**And** the label generates successfully  

*Source: Card request; PluginHive billing flows; PluginHive FAQ on Duties Payment XML*

---

### Scenario 7: Third-Party Billing Flow with Sanitized Address (Regression)
**Given** an international order with Third-Party billing arrangement and a partially invalid billing address  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** the app sanitizes the billing address per Scenario 2 rules  
**And** the Third-Party account information is preserved in the request  
**And** the label generates successfully  

*Source: Card request; PluginHive billing flows*

---

### Scenario 8: Domestic Order Protection (FDX-112 Coordination)
**Given** a domestic order (destination is US or CA)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** FDX-112 guard prevents `getSoldToDetailsUsing` from being called  
**And** FDX-111 sanitization logic is not executed for this order  
**And** the label generates successfully without double-validation  

*Source: Card request; FDX-112 coordination*

---

### Scenario 9: International Order Triggers Sanitization (FDX-112 Coordination)
**Given** an international order (destination is outside US/CA, or international flag is true)  
**When** the merchant generates a label  
**Then** FDX-112 guard does not apply (domestic check fails)  
**And** FDX-111 sanitization logic is executed for billing address fields  
**And** the label generates successfully with sanitized address  

*Source: Card request; FDX-112 coordination*

---

## Priority
**High** — Merchants cannot generate international labels with incomplete or abbreviated billing addresses, causing label generation failures and support escalations; this fix unblocks a common real-world scenario.

---

## Scenario Source Attribution

| Scenario | Source | Reference |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Scenario 1 (Valid Address Regression) | Card request; regression requirement | — |
| Scenario 2 (Field-Level Sanitization) | Card request; AI code analysis | FedEx REST API constraints |
| Scenario 3 (Empty Billing Address) | Card request; AI code analysis | FDX-112 coordination |
| Scenario 4 (Empty Street Lines) | Card request; AI code analysis | FedEx REST API constraints |
| Scenario 5 (DDP Billing Regression) | Card request; PluginHive billing flows | PluginHive FAQ: Duties Payment XML |
| Scenario 6 (DTP Billing Regression) | Card request; PluginHive billing flows
